text:
In Git, branching is a powerful feature that allows multiple developers to work on a project simultaneously without interfering with each other's work. The most common practice is to use feature branches. When you want to add a new feature or fix a bug, you create a separate branch from the main codebase. This keeps your changes isolated until they are ready.

Once your work on a feature branch is complete, you can merge it back into the main branch. Often, teams use pull requests for this. A pull request lets other team members review your code before it is merged, ensuring quality. This workflow, where you branch, make changes, and merge, is the core of effective version control, helping teams collaborate smoothly and avoid conflicts.
